title: Carved maple jam spoon
subtitle: A short-handled seasoned-wood spreading spoon
excerpt: A small spreading spoon in seasoned maple: short handle, flat-bottomed bowl for spreading jam, butter, or soft cheese. Carved from a single seasoned piece, this is a first spoon project that needs no axe work; the blank is thin enough to shape entirely with a sloyd knife.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for seasoned-wood blank dimensions; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the push-cut method on tight blanks.

content.text: A jam spoon is the smallest and quietest project in the spoon-carving sub-category: 15 cm total length, a short handle, and a flat-bottomed oval bowl just big enough to hold a good spread of jam. It starts from a thin slice of
seasoned wood
; no axe work, no riving, no green-wood drying period. Maple carves cleanly seasoned and takes a fine surface without sanding; cherry is the obvious alternative.
The wood

A piece of air-dried or kiln-dried maple: 18 cm long, 5 cm wide, 2 cm thick. A piece of off-cut from a joinery project or a small turning blank works well here. Maple is dense and fine-grained, which means the bowl can be thin without being fragile. Cherry is a close alternative with a warm colour that deepens over time. Sycamore works well too. Avoid pine and other soft resinous woods; the open grain picks up food staining.
Tools
A
sloyd knife
and a small hook knife for the bowl. A
strop
charged with green compound. Board butter for finishing. The knife edge must shave hair from the forearm before starting; seasoned maple resists a dull edge far more than green wood does.
Method
Use
push cuts
throughout; blade away from the body at all times. Seasoned maple is dense; short strokes with a fully engaged edge are more controllable than long passes. Strop every 15 minutes.
Draw the spoon outline on the blank: a 10 cm handle, a 5 cm oval bowl. The handle should taper toward the end and be no more than 1 cm wide. The bowl should be 3 cm wide at the widest point.

Saw or whittle away the waste on either side of the outline. For a blank this thin, the sloyd knife with push cuts removes the waste faster than a saw once you are past the first few mm. Cut with the grain; maple grain runs in one direction and the knife will lift cleanly in that direction and dig in the other. Test the direction with a light stroke before committing.

Shape the handle cross-section with thumb-pivot cuts: hold the blank in the four-finger grip and rotate it as you cut, keeping the blade at a constant angle. The handle should be oval in section, thickest at the grip point and thinning toward the end.

Hollow the bowl with the small hook knife. A jam spoon wants a shallow bowl; 5 to 7 mm deep; not a rounded one. Work in short arcs from the rim toward the centre. The grain in the bowl runs across the width of the oval; arcs that travel from rim to centre travel across the grain and tend to cut cleanly. Arcs from centre to rim go against the grain and can tear.
Thin the bowl back with push cuts on the sloyd knife. The bowl floor should be 3 mm thick and the rim 2 mm. For a jam spoon this thinness is correct; it gives the spoon a light feel in the hand.
Sand through 120, 180, and 240 grit on all faces. Maple takes a particularly clean finish at 240 grit and does not need to go finer.
Finishing

Apply board butter; a beeswax and food-grade mineral oil paste; to all surfaces. Work it in with a cloth, leave for 15 minutes, and buff off the excess. Board butter does not cure; it remains a surface coating rather than penetrating the wood like a drying oil. Re-coat every 4 to 6 weeks with the first wash being the first test: if the water beads on the surface, the butter is still active.
Variations
Cherry: similar properties, deeper colour that improves with use. Food-safe and a good alternative where maple is not available.
Honey dipper variant: carve the bowl into a round knob with grooves instead of hollowing it. The grooves fill with honey and release it slowly.
Kolrosing decoration on the handle: once the spoon is dry and finished, cut simple line patterns with a detail knife. Rub ground coffee or charcoal into the cuts to mark them.
Care
Wash in warm water and dry immediately. Never soak or dishwash. Re-coat with board butter every 4 to 6 weeks.

attrs.intro: Common problems with the maple jam spoon.

items.fix: Strop five passes on each side. If the problem persists, hone on a 1000-grit waterstone before stropping.
items.cause: Edge is dull; seasoned maple resists a dull knife more obviously than green wood
items.symptom: Knife skids across the surface without cutting

items.fix: Change the direction of the hook-knife arcs. Cut from the rim toward the centre, not from centre outward.
items.cause: Cutting against the grain direction in the bowl
items.symptom: Bowl interior tears rather than cutting cleanly

items.fix: Work the neck with short push cuts along the grain direction. Never cut across the grain at the neck.
items.cause: Cutting too deeply into the neck grain or working across the grain
items.symptom: Handle splits at the neck during carving

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
